metadata.summary: Author alert runbooks as Markdown with agent:auto / agent:approval HTML-comment tags on each remediation step so the SRE agent executes only explicitly safe steps.

content.title: Runbook-as-Markdown with Agent Step Tags
content.summary: Author every alert runbook as a Markdown file in the project repo (`runbooks/<alert-name>.md`) with a fixed section spine — Symptoms / Diagnose / Remediate / Escalate — and machine-parseable HTML-comment tags on each remediation step: `<!-- agent:auto -->` for steps the SRE agent may run unattended and `<!-- agent:approval -->` for steps that must wait for a signed human approval. The agent fetches the runbook keyed by the firing alert name, parses the spine, executes only `agent:auto` steps, and refuses (with a structured request to a human) on any `agent:approval` step. Markdown stays human-readable; the tags are the agent's contract.

why.p: Free-form runbooks force the agent to "interpret" prose, which is the leading cause of unsafe actions during incidents (incident.io, PagerDuty, FireHydrant 2025-2026 retros). Tagging each step turns the runbook into a deterministic decision table: any step without `agent:auto` is implicitly forbidden for the agent, eliminating ambiguity. Markdown remains the format SREs already author, so adoption is friction-free, while the comment tags survive every renderer (GitHub, Slack unfurls, Notion). Published case studies report MTTR drops up to 50% once runbooks are agent-consumable, and the `agent:approval` boundary is what AWS Kiro-style "auto-deletion" incidents exploit when missing.

list.item: Teams with more than 10 distinct alert types and a stable service catalog.
Any oncall workflow where the SRE agent should diagnose unattended but never delete or failover without human consent.
Migrating from PDF / Confluence runbooks to a repo-versioned, code-reviewed format.
Multi-tool environments (PagerDuty, incident.io, FireHydrant) where one source of truth must drive every agent.
Prototype services with fewer than 5 alerts — runbook authoring overhead exceeds the win.
Highly conditional remediations that need real branching logic — write a Python operator instead.
One-shot diagnostics during an active incident — write the playbook into the postmortem afterward, not now.

section.name: tagged-step-rule
section.p: The runbook spine is fixed: `## Symptoms`, `## Diagnose`, `## Remediate`, `## Escalate`. Diagnose steps are read-only and always agent-runnable. Remediate steps each carry one HTML-comment tag — there is no third option and no default.

rule.statement: Every bullet under `## Remediate` MUST end with exactly one of `<!-- agent:auto -->` or `<!-- agent:approval -->`. The SRE agent MUST execute `agent:auto` steps unattended, MUST refuse `agent:approval` steps until a signed approval token arrives, and MUST abort the runbook with a `runbook_invalid` error when any remediate step lacks a tag.
rule.rationale: A runbook without an explicit tag forces the agent to guess; published Kiro-style production deletions trace to "the runbook said it was OK to run" prose interpretations. A closed tag set makes the safety boundary mechanical.

example.code: ## Remediate - Scale read replicas: `kubectl scale --replicas=+1 sts/pg-replica` <!-- agent:auto --> - Clear stale connections: `psql -c "SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) ..."` <!-- agent:auto --> - Failover primary: `patroni failover` <!-- agent:approval --> - Drop offending table: `psql -c "DROP TABLE audit_2024 ..."` <!-- agent:approval -->
example.note: Each step is unambiguously routed. Agent runs the first two, requests approval for the last two.

example.code: ## Remediate - Restart the database (only if you're sure) - Run failover when latency is high
example.note: No tags, hedged prose. Agent must guess; safe path is to refuse everything, defeating the runbook.

antipattern.description: Adding an implicit "if untagged, treat as auto" default.
antipattern.reason: Defaults turn forgotten tags into silent escalations of agent privilege; the next contributor will write a destructive untagged step and the agent will run it.

section.reference: Runbook skeleton with all four sections.

section.name: alert-to-runbook-resolution
section.p: The alert's `runbook_url` annotation (Prometheus / PagerDuty / Datadog) MUST point to a file inside `runbooks/`. The agent fetches the file at incident creation, validates the spine, and refuses to execute any remediation when the file is missing, untagged, or fails the schema check.

rule.statement: The agent MUST resolve the runbook path from the alert's `runbook_url` annotation, MUST verify the file contains all four canonical sections, and MUST refuse all remediation (returning `runbook_missing` to the orchestrator) when the lookup fails. The agent MUST NOT invent or "search" for a runbook.
rule.rationale: Lookup-by-search lets the agent pick a wrong-but-plausible runbook from a similarly named service, which is how cross-service blast radius incidents start. A failed lookup is a feature: it pages the human to author the missing runbook.

example.code: # Prometheus alert - alert: HighDBLatency annotations: runbook_url: "github://faionfaion/ops/runbooks/HighDBLatency.md" summary: "p99 DB latency > 500ms for 5m"
example.note: Path is explicit, repo-versioned, code-reviewed. Agent validates spine before parsing steps.

example.code: # Anti: agent searches for "closest" runbook by alert name runbook = vector_search(alert.name, top_k=1) agent.execute(runbook.remediate_steps)
example.note: Vector match silently picks a wrong runbook for a similarly named service; remediation hits the wrong cluster.

antipattern.description: Falling back to a "default runbook" when the lookup misses.
antipattern.reason: A default runbook for arbitrary alerts cannot encode service-specific safety; "restart the service" applied to a stateful primary is a data-loss event.

section.reference: Reference parser used by the agent loader.

template.purpose: Skeleton runbook with the four-section spine and example tagged steps.
Reference parser that returns (diagnose_steps[], auto_remediate[], approval_remediate[]).

see-also.link: Kiro incident retro: missing approval boundary on destructive actions.
